title: Trigonometry
blurb: Unit-by-unit practice for the Trigonometry course — right triangles to identities and the laws.

units.title: Right-Triangle Trigonometry
units.blurb: SOH-CAH-TOA: name the sides, pick the ratio, solve for any missing side or angle — then aim it at towers, ramps, and shadows.

units.title: Special Triangles & Exact Values
units.blurb: Half a square and half an equilateral: the two triangles behind every exact value of 30°, 45°, and 60°.

units.title: Radians & the Unit Circle
units.blurb: Angles measured by arc, a circle where cosine and sine ARE the coordinates — and exact values in every quadrant.

units.title: Graphs of Trig Functions
units.blurb: The circle unrolled into a wave: amplitude, period, phase, midline — and tangent's wilder portrait.

units.title: Identities & Equations
units.blurb: The Pythagorean identity, sum and double-angle formulas — and solving equations whose unknown is an angle.

units.title: Laws of Sines & Cosines
units.blurb: Trigonometry for EVERY triangle: the sine area formula, both laws, and the strategy for solving any triangle from any three facts.

forms.title: Finding a side with sine
forms.skill: SOH: opposite = hypotenuse × sin(angle).

variants.statement: A right triangle has hypotenuse $4$ and an acute angle of $30°$. Find the side OPPOSITE the $30°$ angle.
variants.explanation: The opposite side over the hypotenuse is the sine: side $= 4\sin 30° = 2$.

variants.statement: A right triangle has hypotenuse $4$ and an acute angle of $30°$. Find the side ADJACENT the $30°$ angle.
variants.explanation: The adjacent side over the hypotenuse is the cosine: side $= 4\cos 30° = 2 \sqrt{3}$.

forms.title: Angles of elevation and depression
forms.skill: height = distance × tan θ — the tangent links opposite to adjacent.

variants.statement: An observer stands $12$ m from the base of a tower, and the angle of elevation to the top of the tower is $30°$. Find the height of the tower (in m).
variants.explanation: Height $=$ distance $\times \tan\theta = 12\tan 30° = 4 \sqrt{3}$ m, since tangent links the opposite (height) to the adjacent (distance). Dividing by the tangent instead of multiplying is the classic slip.

variants.statement: From the top of a cliff, the angle of depression to a boat $25$ m from the base of the cliff is $45°$. Find the height of the cliff (in m).
variants.explanation: The angle of depression equals the angle of elevation from the boat (alternate angles), so height $= 25\tan 45° = 25$ m. Dividing by the tangent instead of multiplying is the classic slip.

forms.title: Find the angle from two sides
forms.skill: Match the side pair to sin, cos, or tan and recognize the special ratio.

variants.statement: In a right triangle, the side opposite angle $\theta$ has length $5$ and the hypotenuse has length $10$. Find $\theta$.
variants.explanation: Opposite/hypotenuse is $\sin\theta = \frac{5}{10}$ — a special-triangle ratio, so $\theta = 30°$. Reading the ratio as cosine and landing on the complementary angle is the classic slip.

variants.statement: In a right triangle, the side adjacent to angle $\theta$ has length $5\sqrt{3}$ and the hypotenuse has length $10$. Find $\theta$.
variants.explanation: Adjacent/hypotenuse is $\cos\theta = \frac{5\sqrt{3}}{10}$ — a special-triangle ratio, so $\theta = 30°$. Reading the ratio as sine and landing on the complementary angle is the classic slip.

variants.statement: In a right triangle, the side opposite angle $\theta$ has length $7$ and the side adjacent to $\theta$ has length $7$. Find $\theta$.
variants.explanation: Opposite/adjacent is $\tan\theta = \frac{7}{7}$ — a special-triangle ratio, so $\theta = 45°$. Reading the ratio as the inverted ratio and landing on the complementary angle is the classic slip.

variants.statement: In a right triangle, the side opposite angle $\theta$ has length $4\sqrt{3}$ and the hypotenuse has length $8$. Find $\theta$.
variants.explanation: Opposite/hypotenuse is $\sin\theta = \frac{4\sqrt{3}}{8}$ — a special-triangle ratio, so $\theta = 60°$. Reading the ratio as cosine and landing on the complementary angle is the classic slip.

forms.title: From sin to cos and tan

variants.statement: An acute angle $\alpha$ has $\sin\alpha = \dfrac{3}{5}$. Find $\cos\alpha$.
variants.explanation: Use the $3\text{-}4\text{-}5$ right triangle (legs $3$, $4$, hypotenuse $5$); read off $\cos\alpha = \frac{4}{5}$.

forms.title: Exact values of tangent
forms.skill: tan is √3/3, 1, √3 at 30°, 45°, 60° — then attach the quadrant sign.

variants.statement: Evaluate exactly: $\tan 30°$.
variants.explanation: From the special triangles, $\tan 30° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$. Mixing up $\tan 30° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$ with $\tan 60° = \sqrt{3}$ is the classic slip.

variants.statement: Evaluate exactly: $\tan 45°$.
variants.explanation: From the special triangles, $\tan 45° = 1$. Mixing up $\tan 30° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$ with $\tan 60° = \sqrt{3}$ is the classic slip.

variants.statement: Evaluate exactly: $\tan 60°$.
variants.explanation: From the special triangles, $\tan 60° = \sqrt{3}$. Mixing up $\tan 30° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$ with $\tan 60° = \sqrt{3}$ is the classic slip.

variants.statement: Evaluate exactly: $\tan 120°$.
variants.explanation: The reference angle of $120°$ is $60°$ and tangent is negative in QII, so $\tan 120° = -\sqrt{3}$. Dropping (or flipping) the quadrant sign is the classic slip.

variants.statement: Evaluate exactly: $\tan 135°$.
variants.explanation: The reference angle of $135°$ is $45°$ and tangent is negative in QII, so $\tan 135° = -1$. Dropping (or flipping) the quadrant sign is the classic slip.

forms.title: Combining exact values
forms.skill: Evaluate each special angle first, then add, subtract, or multiply.

variants.statement: Compute exactly: $\sin 30° + \cos 60°$.
variants.explanation: $\sin 30° = \frac{1}{2}$ and $\cos 60° = \frac{1}{2}$, so the sum is $1$. Swapping the sine and cosine columns of the special-angle table is the classic wrong move.

variants.statement: Compute exactly: $\sin 60° + \cos 30°$.
variants.explanation: $\sin 60° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ and $\cos 30° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$, so the sum is $\sqrt{3}$. Swapping the sine and cosine columns of the special-angle table is the classic wrong move.

variants.statement: Compute exactly: $\sin 45° + \cos 45°$.

variants.statement: Compute exactly: $\sin 30° - \cos 60°$.
variants.explanation: $\sin 30° = \frac{1}{2}$ and $\cos 60° = \frac{1}{2}$, so the difference is $0$. Swapping the sine and cosine columns of the special-angle table is the classic wrong move.

variants.statement: Compute exactly: $\sin 45° + \cos 30°$.
variants.explanation: $\sin 45° = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$ and $\cos 30° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$, so the sum is $\frac{\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{3}}{2}$. Swapping the sine and cosine columns of the special-angle table is the classic wrong move.

variants.statement: Compute exactly: $\sin 30° + \cos 45°$.
variants.explanation: $\sin 30° = \frac{1}{2}$ and $\cos 45° = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$, so the sum is $\frac{1+\sqrt{2}}{2}$. Swapping the sine and cosine columns of the special-angle table is the classic wrong move.

variants.statement: Compute exactly: $\sin 90° + \cos 60°$.
variants.explanation: $\sin 90° = 1$ and $\cos 60° = \frac{1}{2}$, so the sum is $\frac{3}{2}$. Swapping the sine and cosine columns of the special-angle table is the classic wrong move.

variants.statement: Compute exactly: $\sin 45° \cdot \cos 45°$.
variants.explanation: $\sin 45° = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$ and $\cos 45° = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$, so the product is $\frac{1}{2}$. Swapping the sine and cosine columns of the special-angle table is the classic wrong move.

variants.statement: Compute exactly: $\sin 90° \cdot \cos 30°$.
variants.explanation: $\sin 90° = 1$ and $\cos 30° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$, so the product is $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. Swapping the sine and cosine columns of the special-angle table is the classic wrong move.

variants.statement: Compute exactly: $\sin 60° - \cos 30°$.
variants.explanation: $\sin 60° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ and $\cos 30° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$, so the difference is $0$. Swapping the sine and cosine columns of the special-angle table is the classic wrong move.

variants.statement: Compute exactly: $\sin 30° \cdot \cos 0°$.
variants.explanation: $\sin 30° = \frac{1}{2}$ and $\cos 0° = 1$, so the product is $\frac{1}{2}$. Swapping the sine and cosine columns of the special-angle table is the classic wrong move.

variants.statement: Compute exactly: $\sin 60° + \cos 45°$.
variants.explanation: $\sin 60° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ and $\cos 45° = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$, so the sum is $\frac{\sqrt{3}+\sqrt{2}}{2}$. Swapping the sine and cosine columns of the special-angle table is the classic wrong move.

forms.title: Coterminal angles
forms.skill: Add or subtract 360° until the angle lands in [0°, 360°).

variants.statement: Which angle in $[0°, 360°)$ is coterminal with $380°$?
variants.explanation: Subtract full turns: $380° - 1 \cdot 360° = 20°$. Stopping after a single subtraction ($20°$) is the classic slip.

forms.title: Arc length from radius and angle
forms.skill: s = rθ in radians; convert degrees by π/180 first.

variants.statement: A circle has radius $6$. Find the length of an arc subtending a central angle of $\dfrac{\pi}{3}$ (radians).
variants.explanation: Arc length is $s = r\theta$ with $\theta$ in radians: $s = 6 \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{3} = 2\pi$. Answering $12\pi$ — the FULL circumference $2\pi r$ — is the classic slip.

variants.statement: A circle has radius $4$. Find the length of an arc subtending a central angle of $\dfrac{\pi}{2}$ (radians).
variants.explanation: Arc length is $s = r\theta$ with $\theta$ in radians: $s = 4 \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{2} = 2\pi$. Answering $8\pi$ — the FULL circumference $2\pi r$ — is the classic slip.

variants.statement: A circle has radius $6$. Find the length of an arc subtending a central angle of $60°$.
variants.explanation: Convert first: $60° = \dfrac{\pi}{3}$ radians, then $s = r\theta = 2\pi$. Dropping the $\pi$ (answering $2$) is the classic slip.

variants.statement: A circle has radius $9$. Find the length of an arc subtending a central angle of $\dfrac{2\pi}{3}$ (radians).
variants.explanation: Arc length is $s = r\theta$ with $\theta$ in radians: $s = 9 \cdot \dfrac{2\pi}{3} = 6\pi$. Answering $18\pi$ — the FULL circumference $2\pi r$ — is the classic slip.

forms.title: Reference angles
forms.skill: The acute angle to the x-axis: 180°−a, a−180°, or 360°−a by quadrant.

variants.statement: Find the reference angle of $120°$.
variants.explanation: $120°$ lies in QII, so its reference angle — the acute angle to the x-axis — is $180° - 120° = 60°$. Using $360° - 120°$ (the QIV rule) is the classic slip.

variants.statement: Find the reference angle of $135°$.
variants.explanation: $135°$ lies in QII, so its reference angle — the acute angle to the x-axis — is $180° - 135° = 45°$. Using $360° - 135°$ (the QIV rule) is the classic slip.

forms.title: Amplitude and period from the equation
forms.skill: Amplitude = |A|; period = 2π/B — divide, never multiply.

variants.statement: Find the AMPLITUDE of $y = 3\sin\left(x\right) + 1$.
variants.explanation: The amplitude is $|A| = 3$: the distance from the midline to a peak. Mixing in the vertical shift $1$ is the classic slip.

variants.statement: Find the PERIOD of $y = 2\sin\left(x\right) + 1$.
variants.explanation: The period of $\sin(Bx)$ is $\frac{2\pi}{B}$; here it is $2\pi$. Answering the coefficient $B$ itself ($1$) is the classic slip.

variants.statement: Find the AMPLITUDE of $y = -2\cos\left(2x\right) - 2$.
variants.explanation: The amplitude is $|A| = |-2| = 2$ — a negative $A$ only reflects the graph. Answering the signed $-2$ is the classic slip.

variants.statement: Find the PERIOD of $y = 3\cos\left(2x\right) - 2$.
variants.explanation: The period of $\cos(Bx)$ is $\frac{2\pi}{B}$; here it is $\pi$. Multiplying instead — $4\pi$ — is the classic slip.

forms.title: Midline of a sinusoid
forms.skill: The vertical shift C sets the midline y = C.

variants.statement: What is the MIDLINE of the graph of $y = 3\sin\left(2x\right) + 1$?
variants.explanation: The vertical shift $C = 1$ sets the midline $y = 1$, halfway between the maximum and minimum. Answering $y = 4$ — the line through the peaks — is the classic slip.

variants.statement: What is the MIDLINE of the graph of $y = 2\cos\left(x\right) - 3$?
variants.explanation: The vertical shift $C = -3$ sets the midline $y = -3$, halfway between the maximum and minimum. Answering $y = -1$ — the line through the peaks — is the classic slip.

variants.statement: What is the MIDLINE of the graph of $y = -4\sin\left(3x\right) + 2$?
variants.explanation: The vertical shift $C = 2$ sets the midline $y = 2$, halfway between the maximum and minimum. Answering $y = -2$ — the line through the troughs — is the classic slip.

variants.statement: What is the MIDLINE of the graph of $y = 5\cos\left(2x\right) - 1$?
variants.explanation: The vertical shift $C = -1$ sets the midline $y = -1$, halfway between the maximum and minimum. Answering $y = 4$ — the line through the peaks — is the classic slip.

variants.statement: What is the MIDLINE of the graph of $y = -5\cos\left(x\right) + 2$?

forms.title: Maximum and minimum of a sinusoid
forms.skill: Max = C + |A|, min = C − |A| — the midline shifted by the amplitude.

variants.statement: Find the MAXIMUM value of $y = 2\sin\left(x\right) + 1$.
variants.explanation: $\sin$ swings between $-1$ and $1$, so $y$ ranges over $1 \pm 2$ and the maximum is $1 + 2 = 3$. Forgetting the midline shift and answering $|A| = 2$ is the classic slip.

forms.title: Phase shift
forms.skill: x − φ shifts right, x + φ shifts left — inside changes act in reverse.

variants.statement: The graph of $y = \sin\left(x - 45°\right)$ is obtained from the graph of $y = \sin x$ by which shift?
variants.explanation: Replacing $x$ by $x - 45°$ moves the graph $45°$ to the RIGHT — changes inside the argument act horizontally and in reverse. Reading the minus sign as a leftward shift is the classic slip.

forms.title: Cofunction identities
forms.skill: sin θ = cos(90° − θ): cofunctions of complements are equal.

variants.statement: If $\sin 10° = \cos x°$ with $0 < x < 90$, find $x°$.
variants.explanation: Cofunctions of complementary angles are equal: $\sin \theta = \cos(90° - \theta)$, so $x = 90 - 10 = 80$. Setting $x = 10$ — treating sine and cosine as the same function — is the classic slip.

variants.statement: If $\tan 15° = \cot x°$ with $0 < x < 90$, find $x°$.

forms.title: One-step identity simplification
forms.skill: Use sin² + cos² = 1 and tan = sin/cos to collapse the expression.

variants.statement: Simplify: $\dfrac{1 - \cos^2 x}{\sin x}$ (assume $\sin x \neq 0$).
variants.explanation: Here $1 - \cos^2 x = \sin^2 x$, then one factor of $\sin x$ cancels, leaving $\sin x$.

variants.statement: Simplify: $\dfrac{1 - \sin^2 x}{\cos x}$ (assume $\cos x \neq 0$).
variants.explanation: Here $1 - \sin^2 x = \cos^2 x$, then one factor of $\cos x$ cancels, leaving $\cos x$.

variants.statement: Simplify: $\sin^2 x - 1$.
variants.explanation: Here $\sin^2 x - 1 = -(1 - \sin^2 x) = -\cos^2 x$; dropping the minus sign is the classic slip, leaving $-\cos^2 x$.

variants.statement: Simplify: $\cos^2 x - 1$.
variants.explanation: Here $\cos^2 x - 1 = -(1 - \cos^2 x) = -\sin^2 x$; dropping the minus sign is the classic slip, leaving $-\sin^2 x$.

variants.statement: Simplify: $\dfrac{\sin x \cos x}{\sin x}$ (assume $\sin x \neq 0$).
variants.explanation: Here the common factor $\sin x$ cancels; cancelling the wrong factor is the classic slip, leaving $\cos x$.

variants.statement: Simplify: $\tan x \cos x$ (assume $\cos x \neq 0$).
variants.explanation: Here $\tan x = \frac{\sin x}{\cos x}$, so the $\cos x$ cancels, leaving $\sin x$.

variants.statement: Simplify: $\dfrac{\sin x}{\tan x}$ (assume $\tan x \neq 0$).
variants.explanation: Here dividing by $\tan x = \frac{\sin x}{\cos x}$ multiplies by $\frac{\cos x}{\sin x}$; answering $1$ from a false cancellation is the classic slip, leaving $\cos x$.

variants.statement: Simplify: $1 - \cos^2 x$.
variants.explanation: Here the Pythagorean identity $\sin^2 x + \cos^2 x = 1$ rearranges to $1 - \cos^2 x = \sin^2 x$; forgetting the square (answering $\sin x$) is the classic slip, leaving $\sin^2 x$.

variants.statement: Simplify: $1 - \sin^2 x$.
variants.explanation: Here the Pythagorean identity rearranges to $1 - \sin^2 x = \cos^2 x$; forgetting the square (answering $\cos x$) is the classic slip, leaving $\cos^2 x$.

variants.statement: Simplify: $\tan^2 x \cos^2 x$ (assume $\cos x \neq 0$).
variants.explanation: Here $\tan^2 x = \frac{\sin^2 x}{\cos^2 x}$, so the $\cos^2 x$ cancels, leaving $\sin^2 x$.

variants.statement: Simplify: $\dfrac{\sin^2 x}{1 - \cos^2 x}$ (assume $\sin x \neq 0$).
variants.explanation: Here the denominator IS $\sin^2 x$ by the Pythagorean identity, so the quotient is $1$, leaving $1$.

variants.statement: Simplify: $\dfrac{\cos^2 x}{1 - \sin^2 x}$ (assume $\cos x \neq 0$).
variants.explanation: Here the denominator IS $\cos^2 x$ by the Pythagorean identity, so the quotient is $1$, leaving $1$.

forms.title: Triangle area with the sine formula
forms.skill: Area = ½ab·sin C for the included angle — don't drop the ½.

variants.statement: A triangle has sides $4$ and $5$ with an included angle of $30°$. Find its area.
variants.explanation: Area $= \frac{1}{2}ab\sin C = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 4 \cdot 5 \cdot \sin 30° = 5$. Dropping the $\frac{1}{2}$ gives $10$ — the classic slip.

forms.title: Cosine of an angle from three sides
forms.skill: cos C = (a² + b² − c²)/(2ab) — the law of cosines solved for the angle.

variants.statement: A triangle has sides $a = 5$, $b = 7$, and $c = 8$. Find $\cos C$, where $C$ is the angle opposite side $c$.

forms.title: The law of sines
forms.skill: b = a·sin B / sin A — match each side to its opposite angle.

variants.statement: In triangle $ABC$, $\angle A = 30°$, $\angle B = 90°$, and side $a = 3$ (opposite $\angle A$). Find side $b$.
variants.explanation: Law of sines: $\frac{b}{\sin B} = \frac{a}{\sin A}$, so $b = \frac{3\sin 90°}{\sin 30°} = 6$. Inverting the ratio (giving $\frac{3}{2}$) is the classic slip.
